entry.title: Displaying Status Icons in Each Row of SALV ABAP
entry.content:
Introduction 

SALV ABAP provides a simple and efficient way to create ALV reports with minimal coding and configuration.
Status icons help visually represent business conditions such as success, warning, and error in reports.
Using icons improves user experience and report readability by reducing dependency on text-based status fields.
SALV supports row-wise icon display using the ICON_D data type, which is widely used in real-time business reporting scenarios.

Scenario

This requirement focuses on visually representing the delivery status of orders in an ALV report using status icons. A green icon indicates full delivery when the delivered quantity matches the ordered quantity, while a yellow icon represents partial delivery. A red icon is displayed for orders with no delivery completed. Using row-wise status symbols improves report readability and helps users make faster business decisions.



Program.

REPORT znp_rp_scn.

TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_so,

         so_id        TYPE vbeln_va,

         customer     TYPE kunnr,

         order_qty    TYPE i,

         delivered_qty TYPE i,

         status_icon  TYPE icon_d,

         status_text  TYPE char20,

       END OF ty_so.

DATA: gt_so TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_so,

      gs_so TYPE ty_so.


FORM fill_data.

  gs_so-so_id = '500001'.

  gs_so-customer = 'C100'.

  gs_so-order_qty = 10.

  gs_so-delivered_qty = 10.

  PERFORM set_status USING gs_so.

  APPEND gs_so TO gt_so.

  gs_so-so_id = '500002'.

  gs_so-customer = 'C200'.

  gs_so-order_qty = 10.

  gs_so-delivered_qty = 5.

  PERFORM set_status USING gs_so.

  APPEND gs_so TO gt_so.


  gs_so-so_id = '500003'.

  gs_so-customer = 'C300'.

  gs_so-order_qty = 10.

  gs_so-delivered_qty = 0.

  PERFORM set_status USING gs_so.

  APPEND gs_so TO gt_so.

ENDFORM.


 *Step 3: Set Icon Based on Condition


FORM set_status USING ps_so TYPE ty_so.

  IF ps_so-delivered_qty = ps_so-order_qty.

    ps_so-status_icon = icon_green_light.

    ps_so-status_text = 'Fully Delivered'.

  ELSEIF ps_so-delivered_qty > 0.

    ps_so-status_icon = icon_yellow_light.

    ps_so-status_text = 'Partially Delivered'.

  ELSE.

    ps_so-status_icon = icon_red_light.

    ps_so-status_text = 'Not Delivered'.

  ENDIF.

  gs_so = ps_so.

ENDFORM.

**Standard SAP icons** come from `ICON` include.
 *Step 4: OO ALV Display (OALV)

DATA: go_alv TYPE REF TO cl_salv_table.

START-OF-SELECTION.

  PERFORM fill_data.

  PERFORM display_alv.

* Step 5: ALV Display Logic

FORM display_alv.

  cl_salv_table=>factory(

    IMPORTING

      r_salv_table = go_alv

    CHANGING

      t_table      = gt_so ).



  DATA(lo_columns) = go_alv->get_columns( ).

  lo_columns->set_optimize( abap_true ).

  " Enable Icon display

  DATA(lo_column) = lo_columns->get_column( 'STATUS_ICON' ).

  lo_column->set_short_text( 'Status' ).
  lo_column->set_medium_text( 'Delivery Status' ).
  lo_column->set_long_text( 'Delivery Status Icon' ).

  go_alv->display( ).

ENDFORM.

Output.

Conclusion.

 
Displaying Icons in each SALV row improves report readability and provides a clear visual representation of data status. Using conditional logic, icons can be dynamically assigned based on business rules, helping users quickly understand each record. SALV automatically renders these icons when the correct data types are used.

entry.title: Building Your First SAP Module Pool: A Step-by-Step Guide to Screen Painter & Menu Painter
entry.content:
In SAP ABAP ,Module Pool was honestly one of those topics that looked intimidating from the outside. Everyone talks about Reports and BAPIs — but nobody really walks you through building your own custom screen from scratch. So when I finally got my hands on it and saw my first custom login screen come alive inside SAP, I knew I had to document every single step. This is that documentation — turned into a guide I wish I had on day one.

What Is a Module Pool?

If you’ve worked with SAP ABAP, you’ve probably used reports and selection screens. But Module Pool programming is a whole different beast — and a powerful one. It lets you build custom, dialog-driven applications inside SAP with full control over the screen layout, navigation, menus, and user interactions.

At its core, a Module Pool program uses two key processing events:

  • PBO (Process Before Output) — Runs before a screen is displayed. Used to initialize fields, set menu status, and populate data.
  • PAI (Process After Input) — Runs after the user interacts with the screen. Used to validate inputs, handle button clicks, and navigate between screens.

In this guide, I’ll walk you through building a complete Module Pool application from scratch — including a login screen, a customized menu bar, subscreen integration, a table view, and even an F4 value help (search help).

Setting Up the Module Pool Program

Step 1: Create the Program in SE80

 

Navigate to TCode SE80, select Program, and give your program a name starting with Z (custom namespace in SAP). Right-click the program in the Object Navigator → Create → Screen.

Step 2: Create a Screen

 

Enter a 4-digit screen number like 0100, 0200, or 0300. Every screen in a Module Pool must have a unique 4-digit number.

Step 3: Set Up PBO and PAI Modules

 

In the Flow Logic tab of your screen, you’ll see the skeleton:

PROCESS BEFORE OUTPUT.
  MODULE STATUS_0100.
PROCESS AFTER INPUT.
  MODULE USER_COMMAND_0100.

Uncomment these lines to activate them. Double-clicking on STATUS_0100 will let you create the PBO module and choose where to store it (use the Master Program).

Customizing the Menu Area with Menu Painter

Step 4: Set PF-STATUS and Title Bar

 

Inside your STATUS_0100 PBO module, set:

abap

SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

Both names must be in uppercase. These link your screen to a custom menu and title defined in the GUI Status editor.

Your moment here — e.g., Did this trip you up? How long did it take you to figure it out?

Step 5: Create the GUI Status (ZMENU)

 

Double-click on ZMENU to open the Menu Painter. Give it a short description and choose Normal Screen as the status type.

Step 6: Build Your Menu Bar

 

In the Menu Painter editor, you’ll see sections for:

  • Menu Bar — top-level dropdown menus
  • Application Toolbar — icon buttons below the menu bar
  • Function Keys — keyboard shortcuts

Click the dropdown next to Menu Bar, name a menu (e.g., DEMO), then double-click it to add entries. For each entry, provide a function code (e.g., SE41) and a short text label. These function codes are what PAI catches with CASE sy-ucomm.

Step 7: Add Application Toolbar Buttons

 

Click a slot in the Application Toolbar, enter a Function Code (e.g., CTRL), then double-click to set:

  • Function text: EXECUTE
  • Icon name: ICON_EXECUTE_OBJECT
  • Information text: EXECUTION

Step 8 & 9: Assign Function Keys and Save

 

 

Map your function codes to F-keys (F5, F6, etc.) from the Assign Function to Function Key dialog. Then click Save → Check → Activate.

Step 10 & 11: Configure the Standard Toolbar and Title

 

 

In the Function Keys tab, fill in the Standard Toolbar entries (BACK, CANCEL, etc.). Then double-click your ZTITLE GUI Title and enter the text that will appear in the title bar when the report runs — e.g., SCREEN PAINTER.

Designing Screens with Screen Painter

Step 12: Set Up Your Database Table

 

 

Go to TCode SE11 and either select an existing table or create a custom transparent table. In the example, two tables are used:

  • ZMP_LOGIN — stores user credentials (PERNR, PASSWORD, WERKS)
  • ZTMP_FY25 — stores customer data (Name, ID, Phone, Address, City, etc.)

Step 13: Write the ABAP Logic

Your main program handles login validation and screen navigation:

abap

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report ZTHIRU_MODULE_POOL1
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
REPORT ZTHIRU_MODULE_POOL1.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  USER_COMMAND_0100  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*

DATA: WA_LOGIN TYPE ZMP_LOGIN.

PARAMETERS: USER TYPE PERSNO,
            PASSWORD TYPE CHAR10.

DATA: CUST_NAME TYPE CHAR30,
      CUST_ID TYPE CHAR20,
      CUST_NUMBER TYPE CHAR13.
DATA: WA_RESULT TYPE ZTMP_FY25.

DATA: DOC_CAT TYPE EKKO-BSART.
DATA: IT_EKKO TYPE TABLE OF EKKO,
      wa_ekko type ekko.

CONTROLS TAB TYPE TABLEVIEW USING SCREEN 0300.

SELECT SINGLE * FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO WA_LOGIN WHERE PERNR = USER AND PASSWORD = PASSWORD.

IF SY-SUBRC = 0.
  CALL SCREEN '100'.
ELSE.
  MESSAGE 'Incorrect userid and password' type 'E'.
ENDIF.


MODULE status_0100 OUTPUT.
 SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
 SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

 case sy-ucomm.
   when 'SE41'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE41'.
   when 'SE51'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE51'.
   WHEN 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
 CALL TRANSACTION 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
*     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'CANCEL'.
     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'DISP'.
     SELECT SINGLE * FROM ZTMP_FY25 INTO WA_RESULT WHERE NAME = CUST_NAME AND NAME = CUST_NAME AND PHONE_NO = CUST_NUMBER.
      IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
        MESSAGE 'Entered ID is not available' type 'E'.
      ENDIF.
   WHEN 'GET'.
     SELECT * FROM EKKO INTO TABLE IT_EKKO WHERE BSART = DOC_CAT.
     CALL SCREEN '300'.

 ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  USER_COMMAND_0100  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE user_command_0100 INPUT.

ENDMODULE.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Module  F4_CUST_ID  INPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE f4_cust_id INPUT.

TYPES: BEGIN OF TY_INP,
       ID TYPE ZMP_LOGIN-pernr,
       END OF TY_INP.

DATA: LT_VAL TYPE TABLE OF TY_INP,
      LT_REL TYPE TABLE OF DDSHRETVAL,
      LS_RET TYPE DDSHRETVAL.

SELECT PERNR FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO TABLE LT_VAL .
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield               = 'CUST_ID'
      dynpprog               = sy-repid
      dynpnr                 = sy-dynnr
      VALUE_ORG              = 'S'  " 'C'
    tables
      value_tab              = LT_VAL.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
* Implement suitable error handling here
  ENDIF.
ENDMODULE.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Module STATUS_0300 OUTPUT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
MODULE status_0300 OUTPUT.
 SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
 SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

 case sy-ucomm.
   when 'SE41'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE41'.
   when 'SE51'.
     CALL TRANSACTION 'SE51'.
 WHEN 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
 CALL TRANSACTION 'ZTHIRU_TC1'.
*     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'CANCEL'.
     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.
   WHEN 'BACK'.
     LEAVE TO SCREEN 1.
*   WHEN 'CANCLE'.
*     LEAVE TO SCREEN 0.

 ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.

The STATUS_0100 PBO module handles menu-driven navigation using CASE sy-ucomm, calling transactions or other screens based on what the user clicks.

Step 14 & 15: Design Screen 0100 Layout

 

 

Click Screen 0100 → Layout to open the Screen Painter canvas. Use the Text Field element to add labels like NAME, ID, NUMBER, ADDRESS, etc. These are display-only elements — they carry labels but accept no user input.

Step 16: Add Input/Output Fields

 

 

Use the Input/Output Field element for fields where data will be shown or entered. Double-click each field and set the name to match your work area — for example, WA_RESULT-COUNTRY, WA_RESULT-ADDRESS, etc. This binds the screen field directly to your ABAP variable.

Step 17: Add a Push Button

 

Create a Push Button, name it DISPLAY, and assign a unique FCTCode (e.g., DISP). This code is caught in PAI to trigger the data fetch logic.

Step 18: Activate Everything

 

Activate all screens, GUI Status, GUI Title, and PBO modules before testing. In SE80, right-click and activate each object.

Step 19: Execute the STATUS_0100 (Direct Processing).

 

Step 20: Test

 

Enter the User & password as in the DB table

Step 21: Test

 

Enter the Values and Click the Display, the fields matching to that input will be fetched from the DB table and displayed here.

Step 22: Test

 

Now you can see the Menu area where your customized tools will be displayed.

Adding a Subscreen

Step 23 & 24: Create Screen 0200 as a Subscreen

 

 

 

Right-click the program → Create → Screen → enter 0200. In the Attributes tab, set Dynpro Type to Subscreen and save. No PBO/PAI modules need to be uncommented for a subscreen.

Step 25 & 26: Design the Subscreen Layout

 

 

Click Layout on Screen 0200. Add an input field (e.g., DOC_CAT for Document Category from the EKKO table) and a GET push button with function code GET.

Step 27: Call the Subscreen from Screen 0100

 

In Screen 0100’s Flow Logic, add the subscreen call:

PROCESS BEFORE OUTPUT.
  MODULE STATUS_0100.
  CALL SUBSCREEN SUB INCLUDING SY-REPID '0200'.
PROCESS AFTER INPUT.
  CALL SUBSCREEN SUB.
  MODULE USER_COMMAND_0100.

Step 28: Add a Subscreen Area to Screen 0100’s Layout

 

 

Go back to Screen 0100’s canvas, drag a Subscreen Area element onto the screen, and name it SUB. This is the container that will render Screen 0200 inside Screen 0100.

Your thought here — Why did you choose a subscreen? What was satisfying or tricky about it?

Step 29: Test Subscreen

 

 

Execute the PBO Module SCREEN_0100 and give the input fields and check whether the sub screen is first working or not.

Adding a Table View Screen

Step 30–35: Create Screen 0300 (Normal Type)

 

 

 

 

 

 

Create a new screen numbered 0300 with Dynpro Type Normal. Uncomment both STATUS_0300 and USER_COMMAND_0300 in the Flow Logic. Double-click USER_COMMAND_0300 to create the PAI module and store it in the Master Program.

Step 36: Reuse the Same Menu and Title

 

In STATUS_0300, use the same menu and title:

abap

MODULE STATUS_0300 OUTPUT.
  SET PF-STATUS 'ZMENU'.
  SET TITLEBAR 'ZTITLE'.

Handle BACK navigation with LEAVE TO SCREEN 1 (returns to the previous screen).

Step 37: Choose fields

 

Choose the fields from the table which you have already used for the subscreen_0200.

Step 38: Create IT, WA.

 

Creating the Internal table, Work area for the table .

Step 39: Write the Loop Logic

In Screen 0300’s Flow Logic, add:

PROCESS BEFORE OUTPUT.
  MODULE STATUS_0300.
  LOOP AT IT_EKKO INTO WA_EKKO WITH CONTROL TAB CURSOR TAB-CURRENT_LINE.
  ENDLOOP.
PROCESS AFTER INPUT.
  MODULE USER_COMMAND_0300.
  LOOP.
  ENDLOOP.

Step 40–45: Add a Table Control

 

 

 

 

 

 

In the Screen Painter canvas for Screen 0300, select the Table Control element from the left toolbar. Draw it on the screen and name it TAB.

Use Dictionary/Program Fields (F6) from the menu bar to pull in fields from the EKKO table — select columns like Purchasing Doc, Company Code, Doc Category, Status, and Created By. Place them inside the table control and adjust column widths.

Step 46 & 47: Configure Column Fields

Double-click each column field. Under Attributes → Program, uncheck Input Field and check Output Only — since this is a display-only table. Rename each field’s Name to match your work area (e.g., WA_EKKO-EBELN, WA_EKKO-BUKRS, etc.).

 

 

 

 

 

 

Step 48: Execute

 

 

 

Process on Value Request (F4 Search Help)

Step 49: Create the F4 Module

 

Create a PAI module F4_CUST_ID that uses the standard function module F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST to provide a dropdown of valid IDs from ZMP_LOGIN:

abap

MODULE f4_cust_id INPUT.
  TYPES: BEGIN OF TY_INP,
           ID TYPE ZMP_LOGIN-pernr,
         END OF TY_INP.
  DATA: LT_VAL TYPE TABLE OF TY_INP.
  SELECT PERNR FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO TABLE LT_VAL.
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield  = 'CUST_ID'
      dynpprog  = sy-repid
      dynpnr    = sy-dynnr
      VALUE_ORG = 'S'
    TABLES
      value_tab = LT_VAL.
ENDMODULE.

 

Step 50: Add the POVR Block to Screen 0100’s Flow Logic

 

PROCESS ON VALUE-REQUEST.
  FIELD CUST_ID MODULE F4_CUST_ID.

This triggers the F4 popup when the user presses F4 on the ID field, showing a list of valid customer IDs to choose from.

Running the Program

  1. Execute STATUS_0100 with Direct Processing (F8).
  2. Enter the USER and PASSWORD credentials stored in ZMP_LOGIN.
  3. On successful login, Screen 0100 opens — showing the customer profile fields and the embedded subscreen with the Document Category input.
  4. Enter a Name, ID (use F4 for value help!), and Number, then click DISPLAY to fetch matching data from ZTMP_FY25.
  5. Enter a Document Category and click GET to navigate to Screen 0300 and view the EKKO table data in the table control.
  6. The custom DEMO menu in the menu bar lets you jump to SE41, SE51, or custom transactions directly.

 

 

 

Key Takeaways

ConceptWhat It DoesPBO ModuleRuns before screen renders; sets status, populates fieldsPAI ModuleRuns after user action; handles button clicks and navigationGUI StatusDefines menu bar, application toolbar, and function keysGUI TitleSets the screen title bar textSubscreenEmbeds one screen inside another screen areaTable ControlDisplays scrollable tabular data from an internal tablePOVRProcess On Value Request — enables F4 dropdown search helpCALL SCREENNavigates to another screen in the same programLEAVE TO SCREEN 0Exits the current screen stack back to the SAP menu

Wrapping Up

Okay, so — we built a login screen, a custom menu bar, a subscreen, a table view, and an F4 search help. From scratch. Inside SAP. Using nothing but ABAP, Screen Painter, and a lot of patience.

Honestly? It's one of the most fun things I've done in this language. There's something deeply satisfying about pressing F8 and seeing your own custom screen load up — with your menu, your buttons, your layout — inside the same SAP GUI that runs enterprise systems worldwide.

Will you hit errors along the way? Absolutely. The uppercase PF-STATUS rule will get you. The subscreen naming will confuse you for a bit. The Table Control loop logic will make you question your life choices for exactly 15 minutes. And then it'll work — and you'll feel great.

My advice: don't just read this. Open your SAP system and build it. Step by step, exactly as laid out. The muscle memory of actually doing it is worth ten times more than reading about it.

And when you're done — come back and tell me how it went. I'm serious. Drop a comment, share your output screenshot, ask questions. This stuff is more fun when we figure it out together. 🙌

entry.title: Sapphire 2026 recap: Joule for Developers Agentic ABAP AI
entry.content:
TL;DR: What you learned on Sapphire 2026 is that in general SAP is evolving from standalone AI tools to an orchestration layer that automates multi-step workflows for business user and developers.
The ABAP platform team is very proud to be on the forefront of this and this was the major focus for all Sapphire ABAP AI sessions. 
In a nutshell we have announced the following key benefits for customers and partners of what will be made generally available in the first weeks of June:

 

  1. ABAP AI now supports Agentic orchestration in Eclipse and VS Code with out-of-the-box ABAP MCP Server lots of great MCP tools.
  2. ABAP AI is available for lower releases of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition >=2021.
  3. Simplified enablement of ABAP AI via SAP for Me and Central Business Configuration with a new commercial model based on AI Unit consumption (->details how to purchase and on-board / AI Units overview).
  4. Faster innovation with Side-by-Side consumption of SAP and 3rd party Agentic IDE, Orchestrators and LLMs (e.g. Microsoft, Amazon, IBM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral…)
  5. Agentic Custom Code Migration accelerates ECC to S/4HANA Transformation -> see the demo below

 

Here is a demo of the first iteration of the agentic custom code migration with mass ready ATC AI quick fixes using Amazon Q in Eclipse (on Sapphire we showed also GitHub CoPilot as orchestrator):

If you want to understand deeper the context of the demo have a look into my Sapphire recap. Here I explain in more detail what it means for you as customer and developer and I also give a bit of outlook to what comes next:

More details can be found here:

  1. More videos on ABAP AI and custom code migration
  2. Sapphire 2026: GA announcement for agentic ABAP AI (Sonja Lienard)
  3. Sapphire 2026: Agentic Joule for Developers - your choice (Simona Marincei)
  4. Sapphire 2026: ABAP AI - Chapter 3: We go agentic! (Alexander Rother)
  5. How to purchase Agentic ABAP AI
  6. AI Units overview
  7. VS Code: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code is now available on the VS Code marketplace! 
  8. FAQ document for frequent questions
  9. Public Roadmap: SAP Help / SAP Roadmap Explorer
  10. Help & Guides
    1. What' New in ABAP GenAI
    2. SAP ABAP AI Discovery Center
    3. SAP BTP Discovery Center (Free Tier Services)
    4. Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
    5. YouTube playlist for Joule for Developers (J4D)
    6. Joule for Developers Help (J4D)
    7. Build SAP Fiori Apps with ABAP Cloud and SAP Joule for developers (RAP120) 
    8. ADT Eclipse AI Capabilities Help
    9. ABAP AI SDK Help
    10. ABAP-1 Help

More high-level insights about Joule for Developers/ABAP AI and ABAP platform can be found here . Here you find more on ABAP- 1,

entry.title: ABAP Code Reviewer
entry.content:
A new feature was developed based on the insights from the Abap Versions Explorer.

This is a Code Reviewer service which can help easily and fast review all the changes, accept or decline it with comments or even ask AI to create Summary or make technical documentation and so on.

Video demo of the process.

Video demo of the process with Ask AI feature.

By default used Code Reviewer feature. 

To use the Anthropic API, we need to set up an SM59 connection to api.anthropic.com. It is also possible to use the OpenAI API. I will publish the solution if anyone needs it.

The current solution can perform code reviews and generate both technical and functional documentation. By chaining multiple requests together, we can map out the entire project lifecycle description.

entry.title: Create a TCode for a Table in SAP ABAP
entry.content:
In SAP ABAP, creating a custom transaction code (TCode) for a table allows users to directly maintain or display table entries without remembering the standard transaction names.

Prerequisites :

Before creating a TCode:

  • A custom table should already exist in the Data Dictionary.
  • Example table: ZVA_EMP_20
  • Proper authorization to create transactions.

Create a Table Maintenance Generator( TMG ) :

  • Go to se11 Transaction Code.
  • Enter your custom Table name (  Ex : ZVA_EMP_20 ).
  • Click on Display.
  • From the top menu  ( Utilities → Table Maintenance Generator ).

  • Click on Table Maintenance Generator .

  •  Click on find screen no.

  • Select propose screen number and click on ok.
  • Now Click on create button then  TMG Generated.

  • SAP generates:

    • Function group
    • Maintenance screens
    • Standard maintenance program

Test Table Maintenance : 

  • Go to SM30 Tcode .
  • Enter a Table Name and Click on Maintain.

 

Create a Custom Transaction Code :

Now create a direct transaction for the table.

  • Open Transaction SE93.
  • Enter custom transaction code ( ex : ZEMP_MAINT ).
  • Click on a Create.
  • Choose Transaction Type is Transaction with parameters (Parameter Transaction).

Now Click on save T-code for Table created .

Providing the Default values also.

 

Finally open table t-code ( ZEMP_MAINT ).

 

Click on Enter.

Now open .

Flow :

SE11
↓
Generate Table Maintenance
↓
SM30 Test
↓
SE93
↓
Create Parameter Transaction
↓
Assign SM30 Parameters
↓
Save & Test

Conclusion :

Creating a T-Code for a custom table in SAP ABAP involves:

  1. Creating a table maintenance generator
  2. Testing using SM30
  3. Creating a parameter transaction in SE93
  4. Passing table parameters to SM30

This approach helps users directly maintain table entries through a simple custom transaction code.

entry.title: Develop RAP-Based SAP Fiori Applications in Minutes with SAP Fiori Tools AI Project Accelerator
entry.content:
Last year, as described in the blog From idea to app: using AI-powered Project Accelerator to develop SAP Fiori apps, the Project Accelerator focused on generating CAP-based projects,

Developing enterprise SAP Fiori applications using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a standardized way to build modern cloud-ready applications. These applications are typically developed in environments such as SAP BTP ABAP Environment and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (version 2602 or higher).

In a significant step forward, the Project Accelerator now supports RAP-based projects In this post, I will explain why the addition of RAP is important and then describe the specific steps  to build an app using this new capability.

The addition of RAP support makes the AI Project Accelerator more relevant for enterprise SAP development by aligning it with SAP’s strategic ABAP model. It enables end-to-end generation of backend artifacts and frontend fully functional SAP Fiori elements application including manifest and annotation configurations. Furthermore, it supports the Clean Core 4-Level Extension Model, as the generated backend artifacts fall under Level A—the cleanest form of extensions.

The AI-powered Project Accelerator in SAP Fiori tools is available in SAP Business Application Studio (SBAS) under a SAP Build Code subscription ( is available).

Application ideas or business requirements can be provided by developers, designers, and business experts in various forms, such as:

  1. Text
  2. Image or UI mock-up (.jpg, .png)
  3. Combination of text and image as a business requirement document (.docx, .md)

The project accelerator enables rapid prototyping of a fully functional application that can be showcased and then extended using SAP Fiori tools or any coding assistants (such as Cline, Claude, and Copilot) integrated with MCP servers (for example, MCP server for SAP Fiori or CAP) for further development.

It generates the complete application stack from scratch, including the CDS data model, behavior definitions, service definitions, OData V4 service, and an SAP Fiori elements application. This follows a greenfield approach, with no reuse of existing business objects.

Getting Started

Now that we understand the AI Project Accelerator, let’s proceed with the step-by-step walkthrough to generate the RAP project using an SAP BTP trial account.

Before we begin, complete the following prerequisites:

  1. Get an Account on SAP BTP Trial (follow step 1 to step 5 and in step 6, choose region US East- AWS) . Note: The trial version of SAP Build Code is currently only available in the US East - AWS region.
  2. Start Booster- Prepare an Account for ABAP Trial (only step 1)
  3. Start Booster – Get started with SAP Build Code

Navigate to subaccount(trial) and verify Instances and subscriptions.

Create a Destination in the SAP BTP Cockpit.

Manually add below additional properties to destination and continue to review step.

  • WebIDEEnabled: true
  • abap_enabled: true
  • WebIDEUsage: odata_abap,dev_abap,abap_cloud
  • HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
  • HTML5.SetXForwardedHeaders: false
  • HTML5.Timeout: 360000
Verify all the details of destination and click on create.    
 

Configure ABAP Development Tools (ADT) and create new ABAP package.

  1. You have installed ABAP Development Tools, latest version
  2. Create new ABAP Cloud Project.
  3. Copy URL from destination properties.
  4. Create new ABAP package Z_<Unique Name> under super package ZLOCAL

SAP Build Code - Create new dev space

  1. Open the SAP Build Code lobby.
 
 
 
Create new Dev space that contains SAP Fiori tools extensions in SAP Business Application Studio.
 
 
Provide a Dev space name (for example: FioriAppDevelopment).
 

Once the Dev space is created and starts running, click on FioriAppDevelopment to open SAP Business Application Studio

Launching the SAP Fiori Tools AI: Project Accelerator

 Go to Project Explorer view and Click on Open Folder, to open your workspace folder (/home/user/projects).

 
Open the Command Palette

Search for “Fiori application” and select “Fiori: Open Application Generator”.

 
For AI-assisted SAP Fiori app creation click on "Project Accelerator"
 
Select RAP Accelerator.
  • Select the destination.
  • Enter the ABAP repository name
  • Choose the package for back-end artifacts generation
  • Provide a transport request (if required)
  • Not needed for local packages
  • Click Connect to proceed.
 

Generating Your First SAP Fiori Elements Application

 The AI Project Accelerator supports multiple input formats. One powerful approach is generating SAP Fiori application from images.

I have a hand-drawn sketch representing a Manage Purchase Orders application. the expectation from the Project Accelerator is to interpret the visual design and underlying business intent, identify floorplan and generate a RAP based SAP Fiori elements application. The generated application should closely reflect the structure, fields, and layout depicted in the image, converting the sketch into a fully functional List Report and Object Page.

Download manage_purchase_orders.png to your local disk and upload it to the Projects Folder. Drag and drop the image to the projects pane on the left.

Click on Choose File and select the manage_purchase_orders.png

 

You can view the generation logs to understand how SAP Fiori tools AI generates your application and if needed, to gather additional details for troubleshooting. // Add as side-info

Click on Generate. The project accelerator will analyze the image and generate a, including back-end OData V4 service artifacts and front-end artifacts. 

For supported features and known restrictions, refer to the documentation

In ABAP Development Tools (ADT), check the destination system and ensure that all backend OData V4 service artifacts are generated and available in the selected package.

 

. 

 

After the generation process completes, the new project (e.g., ai-created-rap) is added to your workspace, and the Application Information page opens automatically.

On the Application Information page, under Common Tasks, click Preview Application.

  • Select start-mock to preview the application with AI-generated mock data.
  • Alternatively, use the start script to preview the application with the backend; however, the backend will not have any data as it is newly generated.

You will then see the SAP Fiori elements application with a List Report (including a filter bar and a table for purchase orders. Compare the generated SAP Fiori application with the uploaded image to verify how closely the project accelerator has interpreted the floorplan, fields, and structure.

.       

Overall, the AI Project Accelerator for RAP can significantly speed up the development of RAP-based SAP Fiori applications, enabling developers, designers, and business users to transform ideas, requirements, or even sketches into functional enterprise apps within minutes.

References:

Generating an Application with the Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI

Why use the AI-powered Project Accelerator from SAP Fiori tools?

SAP Fiori Tools Update: Elevating Development with AI, Usability, and Flexibility

entry.title: Agentic Joule for developer: It’s all about YOUR CHOICE
entry.content:
At Sapphire, SAP made one theme impossible to miss: the future is agentic, the ERP is autonomous, and the SAP Business AI platform is turning into the foundation for how this intelligence gets delivered across the portfolio. Joule for Developers is part of that bigger story.

While some technology announcements feel like feature updates, this one feels more like a launch moment — the point where the plot twist. Over the past year, ABAP AI has been proving its value in the practical trenches: explaining old code, accelerating test work, helping with findings, and making cloud development easier. Useful? Absolutely. But still mostly assistive. What Sapphire makes clear is that the next chapter is far more ambitious: ABAP AI is evolving from a set of smart skills into an agent-led development model — one built around choice: your choice of your preferred IDE between ABAP Development Tools on Eclipse or on VSCode, your partner of choice with your preferred AI assistant and orchestration, your choice of ABAP system from BTP till any S/4HANA version from 2021 up and your choice of consumption. And on top a much more serious strategy for RISE custom code migration.

That is the real shift here. Not just “more AI,” but AI that starts behaving like a coordinated system.

So this blog is not about one more feature. This one is about a full shift of thinking, with the upcoming general availability of the ABAP MCP server, open support for partner assistants and orchestrators, the general availability of ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code, a new consumption-based commercial model, and major progress in custom code migration, SAP Joule for Developers is turning into a developer platform built around one central idea: your choice.

Agentic Joule for Developers arrives with the upcoming general availability of the ABAP MCP server and an open partner strategy built around your choice of assistants and orchestrators

The most exciting signal in this story is the ABAP MCP server as of this quarter: Q2 2026. It opens ABAP development to a wider agentic universe by exposing ABAP capabilities in a way that assistants and orchestrators can truly work with. This is where the story becomes more than assistance and starts to feel like momentum. Instead of one closed experience, SAP is shaping a future in which customers and partners can bring their preferred AI assistant, their preferred orchestration layer, and their preferred model strategy into the flow. The result is a far more elegant promise for ABAP teams: SAP provides the ABAP intelligence, the MCP layer becomes the bridge, and you decide how the full agentic experience comes together.

ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code completes the promise of real IDE choice for ABAP developers

Choice becomes real the moment it reaches the daily developer experience, and that is exactly why ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code matters so much. It turns SAP’s commitment to IDE freedom into something tangible. Teams can stay in the environment that feels natural to them while still tapping into the full power of agentic ABAP. In this new model, Visual Studio Code becomes the creative workspace, the MCP layer becomes the action engine, and partner orchestration brings the journey together from first intent to finished Fiori application. It is a smoother, more modern, more natural way to build. For the first release the new Visual Studio Code is focused on ABAP Cloud but by the end of the year with the promise of the roadmap, the entire ABAP Classic support will be extended from Eclipse to VSCode IDE also.

Meeting you where you are with ABAP AI across private cloud, public cloud, on premise, and BTP

One of the most expected announcement for Joule for developer was not on the features but on the reach: the customers stretch across private cloud, public cloud, BTP and either on premise systems and starting June, Joule for Developers is being positioned to meet our customers where they are, across SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP BTP ABAP Environment, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition releases from 2021 onward. More than that for the RISE customers who fulfill the conditions, Joule for Developer will be available also on their On-Premise systems from 2021 onward. This changes the tone of adoption completely. Instead of waiting for every landscape decision to be perfect, teams can begin from the reality they already have and move forward from there.

Custom code migration steps into a new era with an agent-led toolchain for SAP S/4HANA transformation

If there is one place where agentic AI has to prove it can deliver more than clever assistance, it is custom code migration. And the CCM story now reads much more clearly as an end-to-end journey. It starts upstream with Cloud ALM, where project analysis helps teams understand the migration scope, structure the backlog, and identify where effort and risk are concentrated. That early project view matters because migration is never just a developer task. It is a transformation program. But once the scope is understood, the story shifts decisively into developer execution, and that is where the three CCM execution agents provided by Joule for developer come in.



1. Mass S/4 Custom Code Conversion Agent
This first agent planned for Q2 2026 is about scale. It helps developers take large volumes of custom code findings and move from assessment into executable remediation. Its role is to accelerate the mass conversion work that would otherwise remain trapped in analysis reports and manual follow-up.

2. Clean Core Adoption Agent
This second agent is about clean core transformation. It helps teams to move from technical correction to clean-core through recommendation and automatic fixes. This is planned for Q3 and Q4 2026.

3. Redesign and Modernization Agent (i.e. Dynpro-to-RAP)
This third and most complex agent is about application modernization and redesign. It focuses on the deeper transformation work, helping classic Dynpro-based patterns evolve toward a RAP-based future and turning modernization into a guided execution flow rather than a one-off rebuild effort. This agent is planned on the roadmap for late this year and early next year.

Together, these three agents create a much more structured CCM storyline: analyze the program in Cloud ALM, then execute the migration through conversion, clean-core adoption, and modernization. The timeline reinforces that progression. The first execution step begins in Q2 2026, broader execution capabilities expand through the second half of 2026, and the larger modernization promise continues to mature into 2027. What emerges is not just a better migration tool, but a much more credible migration motion from project analysis to developer delivery.

A new commercial model that lets you choose what you use, when you use it, and how far you scale it

Agentic Joule for developer is built as a choice-driven ecosystem also at the commercial layer. The model is designed for flexibility with consumption-based pricing, clear J4D activation, and differentiated paths for customers and partners. While the free promotion will end on 30th September the teams can work through ABAP Development Tools in Eclipse or VS Code, connect through the ABAP MCP Server and local ABAP MCP tools, at no extra cost, while taping into an agent pipeline powered by an open partner strategy at your choice that includes Microsoft, Amazon, Google, IBM, Anthropic and more. The commercial message is bold and unmistakable: Agentic Joule for developer is not just offering AI capabilities — it is giving customers and partners the freedom to build their own path, with their own tools, their own partners, and their own pace, paying only for the AI features which truly bring value to them. It feels far better suited to the rhythm of real AI adoption, where teams experiment, learn, expand, and refine over time rather than locking themselves into a single path on day one.

Bottom line: the future of ABAP looks less like a chatbot and more like an ecosystem

Put it all together and one message shines through powerfully: Agentic Joule for Developers is becoming a platform shaped around your choice. Your assistant. Your orchestrator. Your IDE. Your landscape. Your migration path. Your pace. And that is exactly what makes this new chapter feel so powerful: ABAP AI is growing up — from answering questions to orchestrating work, from isolated skills to partner-powered ecosystems, and from code assistance to full custom code migration strategy.

entry.title: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code: Your Questions Answered
entry.content:
• General Questions
  • Features and Scope
  • Architecture
  • AI Integration
  • Setup and Getting Started
  • Further Reading

With the general availability of ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code - the ABAP community has a lot of questions. Here are answers to the most common ones, covering everything from strategy and scope to AI integration and setup. 

 

 


General Questions

Q1: What is ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code, and why are we building it?

ADT for Visual Studio Code is SAP's official extension that brings full ABAP development capabilities into Visual Studio Code. Based on user surveys from 2023 and 2025, Visual Studio Code was consistently the most-requested development environment in the ABAP community ahead of JetBrains, Neovim, and others. We are delivering on that request.

The extension is not a rewrite from scratch. It is powered by the same codebase that drives ADT for Eclipse which means you get the same backend connectivity and the same reliability, just in a new IDE.

Q2: Will ADT for Visual Studio Code replace ADT for Eclipse?

No. ADT for Visual Studio Code is an additional option, not a replacement.

Visual Studio Code is known for its lightweight, file-centric workflows. But for larger, more complex projects that require powerful IDE features, Eclipse remains the right tool.

Eclipse remains the recommended IDE for full-featured ABAP development until ADT for Visual Studio Code reaches feature parity. Developers can choose whichever environment fits their workflow and because both are powered by the same underlying codebase, neither is left behind. A good example for this is the ADT MCP server. It runs in the core layer and offers the same MCP tools in both VS Code and Eclipse.

See related blog post: Entering the New Era (ADT for VS Code section)

Q3: Will the extension be open source?

Not at this time, though it is something we would like to pursue in the future. The challenge is that the ADT for Visual Studio Code's extension layer itself is intentionally thin. Most code lives in the ABAP language server, a Java-based component shared with Eclipse that contains millions of lines of code. Open sourcing that would be a major undertaking.

What is under discussion is releasing the Language Server as a standalone component. Once available, it would open up a wide range of possibilities: custom CLIs, third-party tooling integrations, community-built extensions, and more.

For more information about the architecture behind ADT for Visual Studio Code, see the Behind the Design blog post.

Features and Scope

Q4: What features are available in the first release?

The first release is scoped around SAP Fiori development with ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), covering 12+ object types and the essential IDE tools to develop, test, and debug RAP-based applications. Alongside the editor tooling, the extension ships with the ADT MCP Server, enabling coding agents to interact with ABAP systems and perform development tasks directly from the IDE.

For the full and up-to-date scope, see the official documentation.

Q5: What is not in scope, and what is the long-term plan?

The first release is deliberately focused on RAP UI Services development. Two things are explicitly out of scope:

  • Classic ABAP UI programming models (Dynpro, Web Dynpro, etc.).
  • SAP GUI integration. There are currently no plans to embed the SAP GUI.

For anything outside the RAP UI service development flow, ADT for Eclipse remains the primary tool to use until the VS Code extension has caught up to it.

The long-term goal is full feature parity with ADT for Eclipse. That won't happen overnight. It took 16 years to build Eclipse to its current scope but the shared Language Server architecture makes the catch-up significantly faster than starting from scratch. Community feedback will actively shape what gets prioritized.

Q6: What ABAP backend versions are supported?

ADT for Visual Studio Code uses the same codebase as ADT for Eclipse, so it supports the same backend releases. If you are currently using ADT for Eclipse, you can use ADT for Visual Studio Code with the same system. This includes on-premise systems down to SAP NetWeaver 7.3 EHP1 SP04 and above. No special backend upgrade is required.

Q7: What about ABAP Cleaner support?

We know how much the community loves the ABAP Cleaner. Adding support is already in the product backlog and we will invest time in bringing ABAP Cleaner to VS Code via the open source project.

Architecture

Q8: How does ADT for Visual Studio Code work under the hood? Is it Eclipse inside Visual Studio Code?

ADT for Visual Studio Code is running on a new ABAP language server, which is built on millions of lines of client-side code that has always powered ADT for Eclipse. This is the same approach the Visual Studio Code Java extension uses (it's essentially Eclipse JDT running as a Language Server).

This architecture has three key benefits:

  1. Eclipse and Visual Studio Code stay in sync automatically: The same codebase serves both IDEs.
  2. All backend releases already supported by Eclipse work in Visual Studio Code: The existing client compatibility layer is 100% getting re-used.
  3. Object type support scales efficiently: Re-using our existing server-driven development framework means only two rendering engines (one for form-based objects editors, one for source-based editors) are needed instead of many separate custom editors.

See related blog post: Behind the Design

Q9: Why does it use a virtual file system instead of saving ABAP files locally?

Because ABAP code lives on the ABAP server, not on your local disk, a virtual workspace is the only viable approach. There is no way around it given ABAP's remote-first nature.

That said, Visual Studio Code's virtual workspace support is significantly more mature than Eclipse's equivalent (EFS).

Q10: Why is all object editing in ADT for Visual Studio Code file-based, and why does that matter?

Visual Studio Code is, at its core, a file editor. All object type editing in ADT for Visual Studio Code is built around ABAP file formats, which means every ABAP object you work on is represented as a file in the virtual workspace.

This matters for two reasons. First, it aligns ABAP development with how modern developers expect tooling to work. Everything is a file based and diff-friendly. Second, and more importantly, AI tools work best in file-based environments. Predictive code completion, context understanding, and agentic coding loops all rely on being able to read and write files. By going fully file-based, ADT for Visual Studio Code ensures ABAP developers can get the most out of every AI tool in the Visual Studio Code ecosystem.

Q11: Will SAP Joule for Developers features come to ADT for Visual Studio Code?

Yes. SAP Joule for Developers features are coming to Visual Studio Code, starting with the ADT MCP server and predictive code completion, with more capabilities to follow over time.

AI Integration

Q12: Which AI coding tools and agents work with ADT for Visual Studio Code?

Any coding agent that supports Visual Studio Code's virtual workspace filesystem can read and edit ABAP files. Currently confirmed to work with GitHub Copilot, promising results with Cursor and likely more as the ecosystem evolves.

All of these agents can also be configured to use different LLMs, including Claude, for ABAP assistance development.

Q13: Can I use Claude Code or other standalone AI agents with the ADT MCP Server?

The MCP Server for ABAP development currently runs bundled within the ADT for Visual Studio Code extension. This means it only runs while Visual Studio Code is running. For CLIs like Claude Code that operate outside Visual Studio Code, it is more desirable to launch the server independently.

It is under discussion to release the Language Server (and with it, the MCP Server) as a standalone component in a future release.

In the meantime, Visual Studio Code is the recommended environment for using the MCP Server for ABAP development with any coding agent that supports virtual workspace filesystem.

However, technically it is possible to connect the MCP server to any MCP client. See the official documentation for more details.

Q14: Do I need an agents.md file for using AI agents with ABAP?

Yes, in order to get most of your AI agent it is highly recommended to create a custom AGENTS.md file. It allows providing additional project specific context such as coding conventions or how to use certain MCP tools. The official documentation provides a template that helps you get started.

Setup and Getting Started


Q15: How do I get started with ADT for Visual Studio Code?

Open the official extension page on the Visual Studio Marketplace and install the extension from there.
The RAP130 tutorial on GitHub is the recommended starting point. It walks you through building a RAP-based Fiori app from scratch and it is designed around an agentic loop. This makes it the ideal way to experience and get a first feeling what ADT for Visual Studio Code and the ADT MCP Server can do together.

For installation, system setup, and a full feature reference, see the official documentation.

Q16: Where can I find the ADT for Visual Studio Code feature roadmap?

Two resources to bookmark:

  • ABAP Cloud Platform Roadmap — the public roadmap for ABAP Cloud platform.
  • Official documentation — the up-to-date reference for current features, setup guides, and release notes.

Further Reading

Have more questions? Drop them in the comments. The team actively reads and responds.

For deeper dives into the topics covered here, check out these related posts on SAP Community:

  • ABAP Development Tools for VS Code: Everything You Need to Know — scope, roadmap, and community Q&A
  • Behind the Design: How We Transformed the ABAP Development Tools Architecture to Support More IDEs — the architecture story behind
  • ABAP AI - Chapter 3: We go agentic! — Key summaries of ABAP going agentic 
  • Entering the New Era of Agentic AI for ABAP Development — the full Sapphire 2026 AI announcement

entry.title: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code is now available on the VS Code marketplace!
entry.content:
Introduction

On behalf of the ABAP development tools team, I am pleased to announce that the first version of the ADT for VS Code extension is available on the VS Code marketplace! Go ahead, install the extension and use it against any ABAP system that supports the ABAP development tools for Eclipse today.

How to install and use the extension?

Simple.

1. Install VS Code: https://code.visualstudio.com/

2. Open the extension page on the VS Code marketplace and click the Install button: extension page

3. Refer to the official getting started guide for guidance on how to set up your ABAP system connection. If you are already using ADT for Eclipse no ABAP system changes are needed. Otherwise, use our admin guide to configure the system for client access.

4. Check out our tutorial on GitHub to learn how to use the new ADT MCP server with an AI agent to build a simple RAP application.

How can I use the built-in MCP server?

The extension comes with a first version of the ADT MCP server. For security reasons, the server is disabled by default. It needs to be enabled via the extension settings by checking the checkbox (see screenshot). Once enabled, the server will start automatically. The documentation how to configure the MCP server can be found here.

Regardless the chat agent solution you use, we recommend defining ABAP related rules via a custom AGENTS.md file. Our documentation provides a template which you can use as a starting point.

What can you expect?

The first version represents roughly 11 months of core development time. The main focus was on designing and implementing the foundational architecture for the extension and the feature scope for RAP UI service development. Developers primarily working on RAP UI services can now develop the frontend and backend parts of their SAP Fiori/SAPUI5 app in the same tool. The extension also includes development support from AI agents such as Microsoft Copilot. This was one of the most requested features from the ABAP developer community.

In short, how useful the extension is for you will depend on your most common ABAP-related development tasks. A list of key features can be found here.

What comes next?

With most of the foundational work done, the focus is now on adding missing features.

Here are some planned highlights for the next release:

  • Support for RFC logon via password
  • Support of more core development object types: RAP behavior extensions, DDIC tables, DDIC structures, DDIC enqueue objects, DDIC type groups, CDS entity buffers, programs/includes, function groups and modules, ...
  • MCP tools: Unified diff tool for transports, ABAP test cockpit tools, ...

Follow the ABAP Cloud Roadmap if you are interested in knowing what comes next.

Provide feedback and influence our product backlog!

We welcome your feedback! Feel free to use the comment section to let us know which features you are missing the most. Your input will help us prioritize the feature backlog for the next releases.

Still some questions left?

Take a look at FAQ document .

Related articles

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/abap-development-tools-for-vs-code-everything-you-need-to-know/ba-p/14258129
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/behind-the-design-how-we-transformed-the-abap-development-tools/ba-p/14258121

entry.title: Enhancing Customer Master with a Custom Tab Using SAP TAXI Framework (BAdI + SE80)
entry.content:
Have you ever needed to add a completely custom tab to the SAP Customer Master — without modifying standard SAP code? In this blog, I'll walk you through exactly how I did it using SAP's TAXI framework (the BAdI-based screen enhancement mechanism), two BAdI implementations, and a custom subscreen built in SE80(Function code level).

The goal: add a new "Repayment" tab to the Customer Master transactions (XD01 / XD02 / XD03) that displays custom fields relevant to our finance team — all done cleanly, upgrade-safely, and following SAP best practices.

By the end of this post, you'll know how to:

  • Register a custom tab via SPRO.
  • Implement two BAdIs: CUSTOMER_ADD_DATA and CUSTOMER_ADD_DATA_CS.
  • Build a subscreen in SE80 and wire it up.
  • Test the result end-to-end in XD03.

 

Below one is the customer master data. T Code is: XD03(For displaying purpose)

And the box which i have highlighted is the place where new tab will come at finally. The Tab name is AOD Data

Step I) Creating Append Structure to the standard table:

Add one append structure to KNB1 table(Customer master Company code data). Since I'm rendering this logic with respect to company code. If the customer has not provided any company code, It will throw one popup.

Step II) Creation of Function Group:

And then, I have create one Function group over report program. The main advantage of creating Function group over report program is to use global variables. Since we are checking this data with respect to company code, We need to fetch the details of company code and customer in one method and will be writing screen logic in another method.

As I said, we would need one subscreen inside Function group, which I have created below.

The layout for which we will maintain the fields is maintained below.

Here, For XD01, XD02 only we could be able to maintain. So, I have did the same thing i.e., For XD01 and XD02 only I have made those fields are editable since we can maintain the entries. But for XD03 we can only see the data. Same logic I had put it below.

 

MODULE modify_screen OUTPUT. 
  IF sy-tcode CS '3'. 
    LOOP AT SCREEN. 
      IF screen-group1 = 'Z1'. 
        screen-input = 0. 
        MODIFY SCREEN. 
      ENDIF. 
    ENDLOOP. 
  ENDIF. 
ENDMODULE.

Now, I have created one Function Module to capture those values(Company code and Customer number) to configure subscreen and logic. 

Below one is global variables which would need to access at global level.

That's it for Fucntion group. Will see while going on for how to configure those screens and Function groups

Step III) SPRO Customizing: Registering the Custom Tab:

Open T Code SPRO -> Click on SAP Reference IMG

Now Go to the Path: Logistics - General -> Business Partner -> Customers -> Control -> Adoption of Customer's Own Master data fields

Under the above Node:

Step IV) Creation of screen group and Function Code:

In this step we will create the Screen group name and Function group name. The name where you will provide for Screen group description. That name will appear on the Tab in Customer master.

Step V) Implementation of BAdI's:

We need to implement 2 BAdI's here:

  • CUSTOMER_ADD_DATA.
  • CUSTOMER_ADD_DATA_CS

Here, Only one method implementation is enough, i.e., CHECK_ADD_ON_ACTIVE

 

  method IF_EX_CUSTOMER_ADD_DATA~CHECK_ADD_ON_ACTIVE. 
     IF i_screen_group = 'Z1'. 
       e_add_on_active = 'X'. 
     ENDIF. 
   endmethod.

 

Here also, GET_TAXI_SCREEN Implementation is enough and also we will be writing in one more method called SUPPRESS_TAXI_TABSTRIPS.

  method IF_EX_CUSTOMER_ADD_DATA_CS~GET_TAXI_SCREEN. 
    CASE i_taxi_fcode. 
      WHEN 'TAB1'. 
        e_screen = '9000'. 
        e_program = 'SAPLYCUST_XD01'. 
    ENDCASE. 
  endmethod.

 

 METHOD if_ex_customer_add_data_cs~suppress_taxi_tabstrips. 
    IF flt_val = 'Z1' AND i_bukrs IS INITIAL. 
      APPEND 'TAB1' TO e_not_used_taxi_fcodes. 
      MESSAGE 'Please provide Company code to see Repayment status' TYPE 'I'. 
      LEAVE SCREEN. 
    ENDIF. 
    IF i_bukrs IS NOT INITIAL AND i_kunnr IS NOT INITIAL. 
      CALL FUNCTION 'ZSET_DATA_CUST_MASTER_XD01' 
        EXPORTING 
          iv_bukrs = i_bukrs    " Company Code 
          iv_kunnr = i_kunnr.    " Customer Number 
    ENDIF. 
  ENDMETHOD.

After everything, you can see the Tab below.

Now, we can create the data as well and it will store in the table KNB1 and we can display the data through XD03.

Conclusion:

The SAP TAXI framework is an incredibly powerful and upgrade-safe way to extend the Customer Master without any modifications to standard SAP code. Once you understand the three-layer architecture — SPRO (registration) → BAdI (activation + mapping) → SE80 (screen content) — the whole thing clicks into place beautifully.

I hope this step-by-step walkthrough helps you implement your own custom tabs in Customer Master. If you have any questions or run into issues, feel free to drop a comment below — happy to help! 👋

entry.title: By-passing function modules in aunits
entry.content:
Introduction

Unit testing in ABAP has branch wise and statement wise testing in modern SAP development.
While SAP provides powerful features for testing classes and interfaces, even covering branches is important in Function Modules (FM).

Function Modules are difficult to test because:

  • They are procedural, not object-oriented
  • They have no native test-double support like classes
  • They perform external operations (DB calls, RFC, BAPIs, etc.)

To solve this, SAP introduced the Function Test Double Framework, making it possible to simulate FM behavior during ABAP Unit tests.

In this blog, I will demonstrate how to:

  • Create a test class
  • Use CL_FUNCTION_TEST_ENVIRONMENT
  • Create input/output configurations
  • Simulate a Function Module call (function double)
  • Test your application class without calling the real FM


Step-by-Step Explanation

Step 1: Create Test Class with Setup / Teardown

We define a local test class implementing the ABAP Unit framework:

PRIVATE SECTION.

    CLASS-DATA : gv_func_env TYPE REF TO if_function_test_environment,
                 lo_ref      TYPE REF TO zcl_jag_new.
CLASS-METHODS :
      class_setup,
      class_teardown.
    METHODS :
      add_new FOR TESTING.

 

Step 2: Initialize Function Test Environment

In class_setup, we register the function module to be doubled:

 

METHOD class_setup.
    gv_func_env = cl_function_test_environment=>create(
                    VALUE #( ( 'ZFM_JAG_ADD' ) ) ).
  ENDMETHOD.

 

This tells ABAP Unit:

“Whenever this test calls ZFM_JAG_ADD, do NOT call the real FM.
Use a test double instead.”

 

Step 3: Clear Test Doubles

In teardown, we clean the test environment:

METHOD class_teardown.
    gv_func_env->clear_doubles( ).
  ENDMETHOD.

 

Step 4: Create Input Configuration for the Double

The double should respond only when specific parameters are passed.

DATA(lo_test_double) = gv_func_env->get_double( 'ZFM_JAG_ADD' ).
DATA(lo_input) = lo_test_double->create_input_configuration( )->set_importing_parameter(
                        name  = 'NUM1'
                        value = '1'
                      ).

This tells the double:

 When NUM1 = 1 → match this configuration

 

Step 5: Create Output Configuration for the Double

Define what the double should return:

DATA(lo_output) = lo_test_double->create_output_configuration( )->set_exporting_parameter(
                        name  = 'RESULT'
                        value = '1'
                      ).

Meaning:

 When NUM1 = 1 → return RESULT = 1

Step 6: Bind Input + Output via configure_call()

lo_test_double->configure_call( )
    ->when( lo_input )
    ->then_set_output( lo_output ).

This completes the test double behavior.

Step 7: Call the Application Class (not the FM)

lo_ref = NEW zcl_jag_new( ).
lo_ref->add_new(
  EXPORTING
    num1   = 1
    num2   = 2
  IMPORTING
    result = result
).

 

Step 8: Assert the Result

cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
  act = result
  exp = result
).

 

  1. Full Source Code (Ready for SAP Blog)
CLASS zlcl_jag_add DEFINITION FOR TESTING DURATION SHORT RISK LEVEL HARMLESS.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    CLASS-DATA : gv_func_env TYPE REF TO if_function_test_environment,
                 lo_ref      TYPE REF TO zcl_jag_new.
    CLASS-METHODS :
      class_setup,
      class_teardown.
    METHODS :
      add_new FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zlcl_jag_add IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD class_setup.
    gv_func_env = cl_function_test_environment=>create(
                    VALUE #( ( 'ZFM_JAG_ADD' ) ) ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD class_teardown.
    gv_func_env->clear_doubles( ).
  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD add_new.
    DATA result TYPE int1.
    DATA(lo_test_double) = gv_func_env->get_double( 'ZFM_JAG_ADD' ).
    DATA(lo_input) = lo_test_double->create_input_configuration( )->set_importing_parameter(
                        name  = 'NUM1'
                        value = '1'
                      ).
DATA(lo_output) = lo_test_double->create_output_configuration( )->set_exporting_parameter(
                        name  = 'RESULT'
                        value = '1'
                      ).

    lo_test_double->configure_call( )
        ->when( lo_input )
        ->then_set_output( lo_output ).

    lo_ref = NEW zcl_jag_new( ).
    lo_ref->add_new(
      EXPORTING
        num1   = 1
        num2   = 2
      IMPORTING
        result = result
    ).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = result
      exp = result
    ).
  ENDMETHOD.

 

Conclusion: The above code helps to set expected exporting parameters for a function module as user expected and same set as exporting parameters will apply through out the class where ever same function module called multiple times.

entry.title: Unboxing SAP ABAP Tools for VS Code + MCP + a small announcement
entry.content:
This is amazing

The announcement at TechEd 2025 caught my attention immediately: official ABAP Development Tools for VS Code, with Model Context Protocol support baked in. I've spent the last year building agentic ABAP workflows with Claude Code, for my non SAP projects, this felt like a genuine milestone. I unboxed it the morning it quietly dropped. 

An ABAP developer's equivalent of tearing open a new gadget. New smell and everything.

If you're a RAP developer and your goal is to stay in VS Code, this extension delivers. Object browsing, CDS editing, behavior definition syntax highlighting, transport management, ATC checks — it all works. I managed to create full stack RAP Flight Booking application on my ABAP  trial in minutes, without leaving VS Code. Truly fantastic. I was pairing away with CoPilot as my... well.. copilot

The virtual file systems seem to be solid and much more integrated into VS Code than it ever was in Eclipse, and editor was just flying compared to VSCode. It's still lacking a lot of tools, and I haven't found my shortcuts and workflow yet, but that was expected and I think it's a solid first step!

Then I started playing with the MCP, and that's when the fun ended.

The MCP server, as it stands today, is missing some fundamental tools. You can't read source code. You cannot search for objects. You cannot update objects. For an MCP server whose entire value proposition is enabling AI agents to interact with your ABAP system, these are pretty significant gaps. Right now it's a bit like getting a car without a steering wheel — the chassis is there, but you can really only go straight ahead.

I also ran into a wall trying to connect anything other than GitHub Copilot as the AI client. Claude Code in VS Code didn't recognize the virtual file system at all, it actually even refuses to load the extension. Neither did Codex find any files/objects. I'm not sure why Copilot works and others don't — but right now it seems to be the only AI client that plays nicely with the extension. If anyone has managed to get another agent working, I'd genuinely love to know how.

Why I'm still optimistic

Despite the first release being a bit underwhelming, the direction is exactly right, and that matters enormously.

VS Code and its derivatives are the de facto standard IDE today, with a thriving ecosystem of extensions, agents, and tooling that Eclipse simply can't match. It's also already the preferred IDE for Fiori development — so for full stack SAP developers, having ABAP land here too means you can finally stay in one place from the data model all the way to the UI. SAP anchoring ABAP development here is a strategic move that opens the door to everything the modern developer tooling ecosystem has to offer.

SAP has made a clear architectural bet — ABAP development over MCP, model-agnostic, open to any compatible AI client. That's the correct call. The fact that the current implementation is incomplete doesn't invalidate the strategy. MCP as a protocol is moving fast, the community is moving fast, and SAP has a history of iterating quickly on developer tooling once the foundation is established.

Which brings me to something I'm launching today.

The gap isn't just missing tools — it's missing knowledge. Even a fully capable MCP server only tells an AI agent what it can access. It doesn't tell it how to design a clean RAP business object, when to use determinations versus side effects, or which ATC violations actually matter. That knowledge lives in the heads of experienced ABAP developers, and in the ABAP Clean Code Guide.

So I've started an open source project — not as a finished product, but as a shared space to build ABAP best practices together: abap-cloud-skills — a Claude Code skill library for modern ABAP development, built on two layers:

  • Clean ABAP — rules derived from SAP's own Clean ABAP styleguide, converted into AGENTS.md instructions and slash commands that Claude Code can enforce when writing or reviewing code
  • ABAP Cloud / RAP — opinionated patterns for BTP ABAP Environment and S/4HANA 2023+, covering RAP behavior design, CDS layering, clean core compliance, ATC remediation, and testing

The idea is simple: the MCP gives Claude hands, this library gives it a brain.

But no single person has all the answers here. The best ABAP practices are scattered across decades of collective experience in this community. This repo is an invitation to capture that — one rule, one pattern, one hard-won lesson at a time.

https://github.com/matt1as/claude-abap-skills  (launching today , with open issues you can immediately  contribute to — come help build a better ABAP together)

entry.title: The Future of ABAP is Here: VS Code ADT, Zero-Config MCP, and AI Co-pilots
entry.content:
Introduction

While ABAP Development Tools (ADT) for Eclipse has served us exceptionally well, the shift toward modern, file-based cloud development and AI-assisted engineering has made VS Code the preferred playground for developers worldwide.

At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP made a groundbreaking announcement: ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code is officially Generally Available!

This is not just a simple port of Eclipse features; it is a fundamental architectural redesign. By introducing the ABAP Language Server and the ABAP MCP (Model Context Protocol) Server, SAP has bridged the gap between the ABAP world and modern development practices, opening the door to Agentic AI and seamless Co-pilot integration.

In this blog post, we will walk through how this new extension works, how to set it up, what objects are supported, and how you can unleash your favorite AI Co-pilot directly on your ABAP code.

The Game Changer: Agentic AI & Co-pilot Integration

The most exciting aspect of this release is how it enables AI-assisted development. In the past, generic AI tools struggled with ABAP because they lacked context—they couldn't see your custom Z-tables, active classes, or the specific version of your SAP system.

SAP has solved this by building an ABAP MCP (Model Context Protocol) Server directly into the tool.

As I explored in my previous blog, Accelerating SAP Fiori & CAP Development with SAP MCP Server, the Model Context Protocol is a massive paradigm shift that bridges the gap between LLMs and your local development environments. Now, SAP is bringing this exact agentic power straight to the ABAP stack!

What does this mean for your Co-pilot?

Whether you are using SAP Joule for Developers (ABAP AI capabilities) or other popular assistants like GitHub Copilot:

  • Context Awareness: Your Co-pilot can now safely read actual code, find dependencies, and understand your custom objects.
  • Autonomous Investigation: You can ask, "How does BAPI_USER_GET_DETAIL work?" and the AI will find the function, read the code, and explain it without you opening a single file manually.
  • Agentic Loop: The AI can generate code, run ABAP Unit tests, check ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) findings, and iterate until the code compiles and passes all checks.

Supported Objects & Features

The initial GA release focuses on enabling full-stack development for the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) and ABAP Cloud.

Here is what you can build and manage directly in VS Code today:

  1. ABAP Core Objects:
    • Classes and Interfaces
  2. Core Data Services (CDS):
    • CDS Data Definitions, Metadata Extensions, Entity Extensions, CDS Types, Aspects, and Access Controls
  3. ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP):
    • Service Definitions, Service Bindings, Behavior Definitions, Change Documents, and Number Range Objects
  4. Developer Experience Tools:
    • Source Code Editor: Code completion, formatting, navigation (Go to Definition), and hover details.
    • ABAP Debugger: Full support for breakpoints, stepping (step over, step into, step out), and variable inspection.
    • ABAP Unit Tests & ATC: Run and evaluate unit tests and ABAP Test Cockpit checks directly.
    • Transport Management: Search, create, and assign objects to transport requests.

Step-by-Step Setup Guide

Ready to get started? Follow these steps to set up your environment (for detailed system requirements and official prerequisites, refer to the SAP Help Portal Guidelines

Step 1: Install Visual Studio Code

If you haven't already, download and install the latest stable version of Visual Studio Code.

Step 2: Install the Official SAP Extension

  1. Open VS Code and navigate to the Extensions view (or press Ctrl+Shift+X / Cmd+Shift+X).
  2. Search for "ABAP Development Tools for VS Code" (published officially by SAP SE).
  3. Click Install.

Step 3: Connect to Your SAP System

The extension supports On-Premise/Private Cloud systems (via RFC) and Public Cloud/BTP systems (via HTTP).

  1. Open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P or Cmd+Shift+P).
  2. Search for and select ABAP: New Destination.
  3. Choose your connection type:
    • RFC (for On-Premise / Private Cloud)
    • HTTP (for SAP BTP ABAP Environment / Public Cloud)
  4. Enter your system connection details (URL, client, and credentials) and assign an easy-to-remember Alias (e.g., DEV_100).

Step 4: Add Packages to Your Workspace

To start working with your code:

  1. Open the Command Palette again.
  2. Run ABAP: Add Package as Folder to Workspace....
  3. Enter your package name (e.g., ZMY_RAP_TRAVEL_APP) to load its file structure into your VS Code Explorer.

Step 5: Enable the Setting in VS Code

  1. Open VS Code Settings (Press Ctrl + , on Windows or Cmd + , on Mac).
  2. In the search bar at the top, type: ABAP MCP (or ADT MCP).
  3. Look for the setting: ABAP > AI: Enable ADT MCP Server (or Enable MCP Server).
  4. Check the box to enable it.

Step 6: Activate Your AI Co-pilot (The Fun Part!)

To activate your AI assistant with deep SAP integration:

  1. Ensure you have your preferred AI extension installed (e.g., GitHub Copilot or the SAP Joule extension).
  2. Open your AI Chat panel (e.g., Copilot Chat) and ensure it is switched to Agent Mode (Copilot can only use MCP tools when running as an active agent).
  3. Click the Tools icon in your chat box. You will see sap-abap-adt automatically listed and checked as an active tool!
  4. Open the Chat panel and ask your first SAP-specific question, such as:                                                                    "Analyze the class ZCL_TRAVEL_HELPER in my workspace, explain its structure, and generate a unit test class for it."
  5. Copilot will ask for your permission to run the tool. Click Always Allow, and watch the magic happen!

Conclusion: The Future of ABAP is Here

The release of ABAP Development Tools for VS Code marks a massive milestone for the SAP community. By moving to a modern, file-based, and language-server-driven architecture, SAP has made ABAP a first-class citizen in the modern developer ecosystem.

With native support for RAP objects, integrated debugging, and the revolutionary, zero-config ABAP MCP server for Agentic AI, writing ABAP is faster, smarter, and more exciting than ever before.

Have you tried the new VS Code extension yet? What are your thoughts on using AI Co-pilots for ABAP development? Let’s discuss in the comments below!

Useful Resources

  • SAP Help Portal: ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code Guidelines
  • Accelerating SAP Fiori & CAP Development with SAP MCP Server (Previous Post)

entry.title: Creating a Custom F4 Help for Currency Fields in ABAP Using F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST
entry.content:
Introduction :

In SAP ABAP, F4 Help (Search Help) is used to help users select valid values instead of entering them manually. Sometimes, the standard search help does not meet business requirements, and a custom F4 Help needs to be created. In this example, we will create a custom F4 Help for a currency field using the function module F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST . The currency codes and descriptions are fetched from the TCURC and TCURT tables and displayed in a user-friendly selection list.

REPORT zva_search_help_parameter.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Selection Screen
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK b1 WITH FRAME TITLE text-001.

  " Amount Parameter
  PARAMETERS : p_amount TYPE ktwrt,

               " Currency Parameter for Custom F4 Help
               p_wears  TYPE tcurc-waers.

SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK b1.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Custom F4 Help for Currency Field
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
AT SELECTION-SCREEN ON VALUE-REQUEST FOR p_wears.

  " Internal table to store selected value
  DATA: lt_values TYPE TABLE OF ddshretval.

  " Internal table for field information (optional)
  DATA: lt_fields TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF dfies.

  " Fetch Currency Key and Currency Description
  SELECT a~waers,
         b~ltext
    FROM tcurc AS a
    INNER JOIN tcurt AS b
      ON a~waers = b~waers
   WHERE b~spras = 'E'          " English Language Texts
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_list).

  " Display Custom F4 Help Popup
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield        = 'WAERS'     " Return Currency Key
      dynpprog        = sy-repid    " Current Program Name
      dynpnr          = sy-dynnr    " Current Screen Number
      dynprofield     = 'P_WEARS'   " Selection Screen Field
      value_org       = 'S'         " Structured Internal Table
    TABLES
      value_tab       = lt_list     " Data to Display in F4 Help
    EXCEPTIONS
      parameter_error = 1
      no_values_found = 2
      OTHERS          = 3.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    MESSAGE 'No values found for Currency Search Help'
      TYPE 'I'.
  ENDIF.

Output : 

Conclusion :

Creating a custom F4 Help in ABAP improves the user experience by providing meaningful value selections. Using F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST , we can easily display data from internal tables and return the selected value to a screen field. This approach is useful when custom search logic or additional information needs to be displayed to users.

entry.title: Currency Conversion in SAP ABAP Using CONVERT_TO_LOCAL_CURRENCY with Custom F4 Help
entry.content:
Introduction :

Currency conversion is a common requirement in SAP applications, especially when working with international business transactions. SAP provides the standard function module CONVERT_TO_LOCAL_CURRENCY  to convert an amount from one currency to another using the exchange rates maintained in the system. In this blog, we will create a simple ABAP report that allows users to select source and target currencies using custom F4 help and perform currency conversion based on the latest exchange rate.

Code :

REPORT zva_convert_currency.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Selection Screen for Input Parameters
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK b1 WITH FRAME TITLE text-001.

  PARAMETERS:
    p_amount TYPE ktwrt,          " Amount to be converted
    p_fcurr  TYPE tcurc-waers,    " Source currency
    p_tcurr  TYPE tcurc-waers.    " Target currency

SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK b1.
